title: Home Health Care Services

narrative.executive_view: Home health has durable paid demand and a real administrative AI surface, but its high labor share should not be mistaken for replaceable care labor. The exact 2022 NAICS scope is establishments primarily providing skilled nursing in the home with related personal care, therapy, medical social work, equipment and other services; establishments primarily providing non-medical home care are cross-referenced to 624120 [S1]. I therefore score Medicare-certified/skilled home health as the dominant model, while disclosing hospice and payer heterogeneity. AI can reduce OASIS/documentation, intake, scheduling, coding, claims and recruiting work, but aides, nurses and therapists still deliver the product. A 5.5x buy and 8.0x scaled exit are supportable estimates within current Medicare-certified and platform ranges [S8], while CMS's recurring rate adjustments sharply limit long-run capture [S2][S3].
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI changes the wrapper around the visit more than the visit. Scheduling a dispersed workforce, referral intake, eligibility, OASIS and medication documentation, coding review, claims follow-up, QA sampling, recruiting and routine communications are strong candidates. A June 2026 survey of more than 400 home-care leaders reported 91% already using or planning to use AI in operations, but did not separate current use from plans [S4]. A 2025 survey of more than 200 home-health clinicians found 50% used AI personally or professionally and 70% were interested in ambient documentation [S6]. These are adoption signals, not evidence that half the industry's work is automated. Physical care, observation, wound care, medication support, transfers, family interaction and escalation remain human.
narrative.value_capture: Medicare prospective payment lets an efficient agency keep some savings inside a rate period, but the public payer has an explicit rebasing channel. CMS's 2026 rule applied a permanent -1.023% adjustment and a -3.0% temporary adjustment tied to differences between assumed and actual PDGM behavior, producing an estimated 1.3% aggregate payment decline from 2025 [S2]. MedPAC reported a 21.2% 2024 FFS Medicare margin, projected 19% for 2026 and recommended a 7% base-rate cut for 2027 [S3]. That is direct evidence that aggregate productivity and coding behavior can be transferred back to Medicare. Private-pay retains more efficiency; Medicaid and managed-care pricing can transfer more to wages or payers. The selected distribution fraction is therefore only 45%, with no use of durable human demand to inflate capture.
narrative.adoption_timing: Effective adoption is below 50% even though awareness and plans are high. The AxisCare statistic combines use with intention [S4], WellSky's 50% includes personal use [S6], and the national trade association describes cost, integration, workforce readiness, privacy, legal uncertainty and limited small-provider resources as active barriers [S5]. I estimate 30% current workflow-weighted adoption and three years to 50%. The range is wide because scaled agencies with modern EMRs can move quickly, while independent agencies face integration, consent, broadband, compliance and change-management constraints.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The supplied dataset estimates 1,983 firms in the EBITDA band, all in the 100-499 employee class, but it does not split Medicare-certified, mixed-service, hospice or payer models. Mertz Taggart counted 105 home-based-care transactions in 2025; Q4 included three home-health add-ons and named VitalCaring, while its home-care section named Addus and Amivie transactions [S7]. I count three directly observed consolidators as a conservative floor. Current advisory benchmarks put Medicare-certified home health at 3.5x-7.0x EBITDA and multi-service platforms at 6.0x-10.0x-plus [S8]. The selected 5.5x buy and 8.0x exit fit those ranges but require clean OASIS, billing, compliance, referral concentration and management diligence; hospice economics must be separated rather than used to inflate the multiple.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is durable because the covered service is skilled, physical care in the home, not generic information. MedPAC recorded 2.7 million FFS beneficiaries, $16.0 billion of 2024 spending and more than 12,000 Medicare-certified agencies; 97% of beneficiaries lived in a ZIP code served by at least two agencies [S3]. AI may help triage, monitor and document, and payers may reduce visits or rates, but it does not perform wound care, bathing, transfers, medication support or a skilled nursing assessment in the patient's home. The Alliance also notes growing demand and strong preferences for home-based care while emphasizing the distinct, uncontrolled home environment [S5]. The economic risk is rate capture and labor availability, not disappearance of paid demand.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The first-order risk is scope and payer heterogeneity. Official NAICS excludes establishments primarily engaged in non-medical home care, while still allowing personal-care services when bundled with skilled home health; it also lists in-home hospice as an example [S1]. The 4-digit OEWS role mix and the dataset's broad firm counts can therefore blend models with different wages, margins and multiples. Adoption evidence is vendor-sponsored and often mixes use, plans, interest, personal use and professional deployment [S4][S6]. Medicare margin evidence is strong but applies to FFS freestanding HHAs, not Medicaid personal care, MA contracts or private pay [S3]. The selected valuation pair uses skilled-home-health and platform ranges, not higher hospice multiples [S8]. Owners over 60 and succession shortage are weakly evidenced, so they are estimated conservatively and shortage is not asserted.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.what_it_supports: Exact 621610 scope, illustrative examples and cross-reference excluding establishments primarily engaged in non-medical home care to 624120.

sources.title: Calendar Year 2026 Home Health Prospective Payment System Final Rule
sources.what_it_supports: PDGM 30-day payment mechanics, 2026 permanent and temporary adjustments and estimated aggregate payment change.

sources.title: MedPAC March 2026 Report, Chapter 8: Home Health Care Services
sources.what_it_supports: Medicare beneficiary, spending, agency-supply, access, margin, visit and payment-recommendation evidence.

sources.title: AxisCare Independent Survey on AI Adoption in Home Care
sources.what_it_supports: June 2026 survey of more than 400 leaders reporting 91% using or planning operational AI and reported efficiency outcomes among users.

sources.title: National Alliance for Care at Home Response on AI in Health Care
sources.what_it_supports: Home-setting demand, privacy and consent issues, interoperability gaps, cost, workforce-readiness and legal barriers to AI deployment.

sources.title: WellSky 2025 Home Health AI Clinician Survey
sources.what_it_supports: Survey of more than 200 home-health clinicians: 50% personal or professional AI use, 70% interest in documentation tools and retention relevance.

sources.title: Q4 2025 Home-Based Care M&A Report
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 transaction volume, Q4 home-health/home-care deal activity and named sponsor-backed or strategic consolidators.

sources.title: Home Care Business EBITDA Multiples: 2026 Benchmark Report
sources.what_it_supports: Segment-specific advisory ranges: 3.5x-7.0x for Medicare-certified home health and 6.0x-10.0x-plus for multi-service platforms.

sources.title: Annual Business Survey 2022 Characteristics of Business Owners
sources.what_it_supports: Broad Census employer-business owner-age tables used only as a low-quality proxy anchor; not a 621610 owner-age statistic.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621610 ($71,424,773,339) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621610 ($113,350,417,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Home Health and Personal Care Aides
Licensed Practical and Licensed Vocational Nurses

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $72,294 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621610); class <5: 11,187 firms, avg revenue/firm $242,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $38,310 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,386 firms, avg revenue/firm $660,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $104,317 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,828 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,118,957 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $176,795 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 7,049 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,771,668 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $437,923 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,983 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,545,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,666,177 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 765 firms, avg revenue/firm $83,228,652 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,150,127 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,983
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Hands-on field care persists; replacement concentrates in documentation, intake, scheduling, coding, claims, QA, recruiting and management reporting. The weighted role estimate is about 12.3% of wages.
caveats: RN documentation savings may raise capacity or retention rather than remove positions.
The 17.66% residual combines schedulers, coders, recruiters and marketers with very different automation exposure.

breakdown_by_role.role: Home Health and Personal Care Aides
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Bathing, transfers, meal support and companionship are physical; only a negligible reporting slice is replaceable.
OASIS, care-plan and visit documentation can automate materially, but skilled observation and treatment remain human.

breakdown_by_role.role: Licensed Practical and Licensed Vocational Nurses
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Hands-on nursing persists; limited documentation and routine reporting can be automated.
Direct physical assistance remains the service, with little replaceable administrative work.
Home therapy visits remain physical and licensed; AI can draft documentation and exercises.
QA review, utilization reporting and scheduling oversight automate; leadership, compliance accountability and referral management persist.
Resource matching and routine documentation automate; counseling, family coordination and escalation remain human.
Data entry, document routing, referral intake and claims support are strong automation candidates.
Scheduling, communications, eligibility and standard record workflows are highly automatable.
Home OT assessment and treatment persist; only documentation and routine educational drafting automate.
Residual roles likely include scheduling, coding and recruiting with high exposure plus relationship and clinical roles with low exposure.

pi_dist.rationale: Agencies keep efficiency between rate resets, but CMS has repeatedly adjusted PDGM payments for observed behavior and MedPAC recommends further cuts when payments exceed costs.
caveats: Strong Medicare evidence does not directly quantify Medicaid, MA or private-pay pass-through; the selected value represents the skilled-home-health base case.

pi_moat.rationale: Certification, quality record, referral links and local caregiver density create friction, but MedPAC's wide beneficiary choice and a fragmented acquisition market constrain pricing power.
caveats: Certificate-of-need and licensure barriers vary by state, and personal-care operations outside exact 621610 may have lower entry barriers.

t50_years.rationale: Strong stated plans and clinician interest support relatively fast diffusion, while integration, privacy, consent and resource constraints keep effective adoption below the headline survey signals.
caveats: Surveys do not report a representative six-digit, workflow-weighted effective-adoption measure.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Available evidence shows widespread use, planning and interest but cannot isolate current production use; 30% is a conservative bridge below the 50% personal-or-professional clinician-use statistic.
caveats: AxisCare combines current use with plans, WellSky includes personal use, and both are vendor-sponsored.

historical_analogs.value: EHR/OASIS digitization, EVV, telehealth and PDGM workflow adaptation
historical_analogs.rationale: Past mandated workflow changes and health-IT diffusion show scaled agencies adapting faster while smaller agencies face integration and training costs.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: 3-7 year uneven diffusion across agency size and payer model
caveats: Mandated EVV and payment-model changes are imperfect analogs for voluntary AI investment.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: Conservative estimate anchored only to broad Census employer-business owner-age tables; no reopened source supplied a six-digit home-health owner-age statistic.
caveats: Broad employer-business proxy, not 621610-specific and not necessarily weighted by control ownership.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened source directly documented an industry-specific succession shortage, so the selection is conservatively false.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false; true only with direct industry evidence
caveats: Active M&A and aging portfolio companies do not by themselves prove an owner succession shortage.

active_consolidators.rationale: Conservative count of three directly named active buyers or platforms in the reopened Q4 report: VitalCaring, Addus and Amivie.
caveats: Addus and Amivie examples include home care, which may fall outside exact 621610 when primarily non-medical; the count is a conservative home-based-care buyer floor.

buy_mult.rationale: 5.5x sits in the premium portion of the reopened 3.5x-7.0x Medicare-certified home-health range and fits a clean $1-10M EBITDA operator rather than hospice.
caveats: Advisory benchmark, not a complete closed-deal tape; quality scores, CON status, payer mix, compliance and earnouts can move price materially.

exit_mult.rationale: 8.0x is inside the reopened 6.0x-10.0x-plus multi-service platform range and requires management depth, geographic density, quality and payer diversification.
caveats: The platform range can include broader service lines; hospice multiples are excluded from the base case, and CMS rate risk can compress exit pricing.

pricing_structure.value: Medicare PDGM 30-day prospective payments for skilled home health, MA negotiated rates, state-set Medicaid/HCBS rates and private-pay hourly arrangements
pricing_structure.rationale: Official scope and payment sources support the skilled-home-health base; other payer lines are disclosed to reflect mixed agencies without treating primarily non-medical home care as exact 621610.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Prospective episode, per-visit, state-set hourly and private-pay hourly models
caveats: The payer mix of the supplied EBITDA-band firms is not reported, and exact 621610 excludes establishments primarily providing non-medical home care.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Current effective adoption is estimated below 50%, so a positive three-year t50 is required and consistent with the evidence.

terminal_value.justification: Skilled nursing, aide and therapy services must still be delivered in the home; AI affects workflow, visit intensity and payer economics rather than eliminating paid care.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Official Census scope and CMS/MedPAC payment claims are direct; owner age, adoption, moat, active-buyer count and multiples are labeled estimates with scope caveats.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: CMS's permanent and temporary PDGM adjustments directly demonstrate payer clawback; MA and Medicaid transfer are treated as heterogeneous and not quantified without direct data.

dataset_mismatch.description: Labor share uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization factor; OEWS role mix is 4-digit 6216 rather than exact 621610.
sensitivity: A lower true labor share reduces V proportionally.
A skilled-nursing-heavy exact role mix changes the RN and aide weights; a broader home-care mix would overstate exact 621610 exposure.

market_boundary.selection_basis: The official 2022 definition says establishments are primarily engaged in skilled nursing in the home and cross-references primarily non-medical home care to 624120; CMS and MedPAC provide the strongest direct economics for the selected dominant model.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Moves A from 5.0 to 2.0 and changes realization timing materially.
Can compress or expand M substantially; CMS rate risk and platform quality are decisive.
Changes revenue-level labor-cost uplift by roughly seven percentage points before capture.

reviewer_note: Exact NAICS scope was applied: primarily non-medical home care is outside 621610. Skilled-home-health multiples, not hospice or generic home-care multiples, anchor the selected buy and exit values.
